concept_name: Gravitational Field of Spherical Shell and Solid Sphere
locked_facts: Uniform spherical shell: g = 0 inside, g = GM/r² outside (as if all mass at centre)
Uniform solid sphere outside: g = GM/r² (same as point mass)
Uniform solid sphere inside (r < R): g = GM r/R³ (field ∝ r, linear increase)
At the surface: g = GM/R² (same for shell and solid sphere)
Shell theorem: a uniform shell attracts an external point as if all mass is at its centre
Shell theorem: a uniform shell exerts ZERO net force on any mass inside it
These results follow directly from Gauss's law for gravity
truth_statements: Inside a uniform spherical shell the gravitational field is exactly zero — every element of the shell pulls equally in all directions
Outside a uniform sphere or shell the field is identical to that of a point mass at the centre
Inside a uniform solid sphere the gravitational field increases linearly with distance from the centre
The shell theorem is the gravitational analogue of the result for a charged conducting shell
Newton proved the shell theorem to justify treating planets as point masses for orbital calculations

aha_moment.what_to_show: Cross-section of a shell with a mass inside. Opposite shell elements are drawn: the closer element subtends a smaller solid angle but is nearer (stronger pull), the farther element subtends a larger solid angle but is weaker. The two effects EXACTLY cancel. Net force = 0.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two opposing cone-shaped elements of the shell with force arrows that exactly cancel
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think the closer wall should pull harder — seeing the angle subtended compensate for distance makes the zero-field result intuitive

simulation_states.label: Shell: outside
simulation_states.description: A spherical shell with mass M. A test mass outside at distance r. Field g = GM/r² pointing toward centre — identical to a point mass at centre.
simulation_states.emphasis: From outside, a shell looks exactly like a point mass

simulation_states.label: Shell: inside
simulation_states.description: Test mass placed inside the shell. Forces from opposing shell elements drawn. They cancel exactly. Net field = 0. Mass floats freely inside.
simulation_states.emphasis: Inside the shell: g = 0. No net gravitational force from the shell.

simulation_states.label: Solid sphere: inside
simulation_states.description: Solid uniform sphere. At distance r from centre, only mass within radius r contributes (M' = M(r/R)³). g = GM'r/R³ = GMr/R³. Field increases linearly from centre to surface.
simulation_states.emphasis: Inside solid sphere: g ∝ r (linear increase). Only enclosed mass matters.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student moves test mass from centre through surface to outside. g vs r graph builds. Toggle between shell (zero inside) and solid sphere (linear inside). Both are GM/r² outside.
simulation_states.emphasis: Compare shell (zero inside) vs solid sphere (linear inside). Same outside!

common_misconceptions.belief: The closer wall of a shell pulls harder, so the mass inside is pulled toward it
common_misconceptions.correction: The closer wall is nearer but subtends a smaller solid angle. The far wall is farther but subtends a larger solid angle. These effects exactly cancel — the net force is zero everywhere inside.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Draw opposing cone elements and show exact force cancellation

common_misconceptions.belief: Inside a solid sphere, g keeps increasing toward the centre
common_misconceptions.correction: Inside a uniform solid sphere, g increases linearly from 0 at the centre to GM/R² at the surface. At the centre, symmetry gives g = 0. The mass above any point acts like a shell (zero contribution).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show g increasing linearly from zero at centre to maximum at surface
